lens.included_activities: Independent US paper mills that make paper from purchased or internally produced pulp and repeatedly supply external tissue, packaging-paper, printing-writing, technical, or specialty-paper customers, with transferable standalone operations in the target band.
lens.excluded_activities: Paperboard-only mills, converted-paper plants that do not make paper, captive internal mills, dormant or closure assets, project shells, non-control interests, and operations outside the target band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is primarily tonnage, roll, sheet, or grade-based product sales under spot, formula, and recurring supply arrangements; it is repeat B2B supply rather than an outsourced service fee.
lens.deviation_from_default: Paper milling is capital-intensive product manufacturing, often integrated or captive. The lens is narrowed to independent mills with recurring external supply relationships so eligibility is coherent across disparate paper grades.

a.bridge: BLS occupational data combine pulp, paper, and paperboard mills at NAICS 322100 rather than isolating 322120. I assign AI exposure to maintenance planning, process-event analysis, production scheduling, quality and laboratory documentation, procurement, customer specifications, compliance reporting, sales, and administration, with low language-model substitution for continuous-process operators and hands-on maintenance. Paper mills may have more converting, quality, and customer-grade work than pulp mills, which could make the combined proxy modestly understate exposure.
a.rationale: Combined mills are 51.68% production employment and 13.00% installation, maintenance, and repair, including 8.06% industrial machinery mechanics. Paper formation, dewatering, drying, finishing, and equipment care are physical and continuous. The exposed minority lies in higher-wage planning, troubleshooting knowledge, grade-change support, documentation, and commercial work.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and current-automation inventory from tissue, packaging-paper, printing-writing, and specialty-paper mills would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No six-digit paper-mill occupational mix was located.
Existing distributed-control, quality-control, and predictive-maintenance automation is not separated from not-yet-automated opportunity.

rho.rationale: Maintenance copilots, grade and specification knowledge, scheduling, procurement, quality records, customer documentation, and environmental reporting can be implemented within five years. Legacy mill systems, 24/7 uptime, cyber and process safety, sparse abnormal-event data, union workflows, and human accountability constrain realized deployment. Direct closed-loop process control should remain outside the base implementation assumption.
rho.change_evidence: Mill pilots measuring accepted recommendations, planning hours, downtime, grade-change losses, quality rejects, energy and fiber yield, safety, and permit deviations over a full year would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No industry-specific five-year AI implementation study was located.
Benefits from conventional automation and advanced process control must be separated from AI labor substitution.

e.rationale: Only independent mills with repeat external customers, transferable environmental permits and operating systems, and target-band normalized EBITDA fit the lens. Capital intensity, vertical integration, captive supply, grade-specific assets, and corporate ownership substantially reduce eligibility. The dataset supplies no defensible LMM firm count, so this share cannot establish absolute availability.
e.change_evidence: A complete US mill map linking grade, ownership, integration/captive status, external customer share, normalized EBITDA, permits, liabilities, and standalone transferability would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The frozen dataset explicitly has no defensible n value; this eligibility share must not be converted into a firm count.
The code includes integrated mills and mills that also convert their own paper.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer plans for US employer-business owners across industries, not paper-mill control transfers or completions. I discount the 22% employer intention rate for capital intensity, corporate ownership, permit liabilities, grade-specific assets, and the prevalence of portfolio, conversion, or closure transactions that do not preserve an eligible LMM operation. The general survey likely overstates qualifying probability.
s5.rationale: General employer-owner succession pressure is meaningful, but mill ownership changes depend more on strategic portfolio decisions, competitiveness, environmental exposure, energy and fiber costs, and end-grade demand than on a small owner's retirement. Completed eligible transfers should be below the general employer intention rate.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed paper-mill control transactions, closures, conversions, and permit ownership changes from Fastmarkets/RISI, PitchBook, Capital IQ, state environmental records, and industry advisers would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are weak evidence for mill transactions.
Asset sales, internal reorganizations, and conversions may not qualify as transferable operating firms.

q.rationale: Paper is sold to sophisticated industrial buyers, distributors, printers, converters, and consumer-product firms through competitive grade and service specifications. Mills can retain some savings through existing pricing periods, reliability, quality, and specialty grades, but benchmark pricing, contract resets, imports, and excess capacity pass much of the benefit through. Commodity grades retain less than differentiated technical or specialty papers.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, benchmark and formula clauses, grade-level price realization, contribution margins, rebates, service penalties, and cycle-spanning price/cost bridges would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No public study directly isolates pass-through of AI-derived productivity.
Fiber, energy, chemicals, freight, and downtime volatility can dominate small labor savings.

d5.bridge: AF&PA reports current production and capacity by broad downstream grade, and BLS projects combined pulp, paper, and paperboard output; neither is a constant-quality five-year forecast for NAICS 322120 or the eligible independent-mill basket. I combine severe printing-writing capacity contraction with resilient tissue and packaging-paper performance and use combined-industry roughly flat real output as a weak anchor. Grade mix determines the direction of error.
d5.rationale: US paper and paperboard production fell 3.7% in 2025. Printing-writing capacity fell 13.9% that year and from nearly 18 million tons in 2015 to 7.7 million tons, while packaging paper grew 1.7% and tissue remained near 7.8 million tons. BLS projects combined mill real output roughly flat through 2034. A moderate five-year decline is the center, but specialty, tissue, and packaging-oriented mills can outperform sharply.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year demand and capacity by paper grade, mill-level order books, customer contract renewals, import/export balances, substitution rates, and announced closures/conversions would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Paperboard demand is included in some cited aggregates but excluded from this six-digit title.
The average is especially sensitive to the eligible firm's grade mix; printing-writing and tissue should not share one point forecast in diligence.

o.rationale: Paper is a physical material made by mixing fiber, water, and additives, forming and dewatering a sheet, drying, finishing, testing, and managing wastewater. Software can automate ordering, specifications, and some control support but cannot eliminate the accountable mill operator. Limited downside reflects customer integration, material substitution, and converters sourcing directly from other mills, not pure software replacement.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customer-owned mill capacity, autonomous mill operating models without accountable organizations, distributed on-demand paper production, or rapid non-paper material substitution would materially change the estimate.
caveats: This measures operator necessity rather than employment intensity.
Quantity loss from digitization or material substitution belongs in d5.

narrative.executive_view: Paper mills offer bounded AI gains in maintenance, planning, quality, compliance, and commercial workflows, but they remain capital-intensive physical manufacturers with competitive industrial pricing. The missing dataset firm count prevents any defensible absolute availability conclusion, and only independent mills with repeat external customers fit the lens.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can synthesize maintenance history, assist work orders and troubleshooting, support grade changes and scheduling, draft quality and permit records, reconcile specifications, and improve procurement and customer service. It should not be assumed to replace continuous-process operators, technicians, or accountable environmental personnel.
narrative.value_capture: Existing contracts, specialty grades, uptime, and quality can shelter some benefit. Over five years, formula pricing, bids, capacity competition, imports, and buyer negotiation should share a substantial portion, with commodity grades capturing less than technical or specialty products.
narrative.firm_availability: Eligibility requires a standalone independent mill, repeat external sales, target-band economics, transferable permits and liabilities, and durable management. The frozen dataset has no defensible LMM firm count, and general small-business succession evidence cannot repair that gap.
narrative.demand_durability: Tissue, packaging paper, and specialties are relatively resilient, while printing-writing continues structural contraction. Grade mix dominates the outlook; the aggregate base is moderate decline, not a single thesis that applies to every paper mill.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are the missing firm-count denominator, capital and maintenance intensity, environmental liabilities, energy and fiber costs, mill closures and conversions, legacy controls, commodity pricing, grade-specific demand, and composition error from combined four-digit data.
narrative.principal_driver: Complex 24/7 mills generate high-value maintenance, quality, scheduling, and compliance knowledge work that AI can augment while customers continue to need physical paper.
narrative.principal_weakness: Absolute firm availability is unknowable from the frozen dataset, and structural decline in printing-writing plus competitive pricing limits a broad roll-up thesis.

sources.title: Pulp, Paper, and Paperboard Mills - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Combined-mill occupational mix: 51.68% production and 13.00% installation, maintenance, and repair, including 8.06% industrial machinery mechanics.

sources.title: NPDES Permit No. MA0001716 Fact Sheet
sources.supports: Paper production process: fiber, water, and additives are pulped, fed to a paper machine, formed, and dewatered from about 95% water to under 5%, with wastewater treatment.

sources.title: Monadnock Paper Mills Draft NPDES Permit NH0000230
sources.supports: Concrete paper-mill operating accountability: continuous effluent-flow monitoring and recurring pH, BOD, suspended-solids, nutrient, metal, and PFAS sampling requirements.

sources.title: NAICS Definition: Paper Mills
sources.supports: Industry scope: establishments making paper from pulp, whether they manufacture or purchase pulp and whether they also convert the paper they make.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Cross-industry succession proxy: 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and 22% planned a five-year sale or transfer.

sources.title: AF&PA Releases 66th Annual Paper Industry Capacity and Fiber Consumption Survey
sources.supports: 2025 grade and capacity evidence: total paper/paperboard production down 3.7%, printing-writing capacity down 13.9%, packaging paper up 1.7%, and tissue near 7.8 million tons.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry
sources.supports: BLS projects combined NAICS 322100 real output from 63.9 to 63.1 billion chained 2017 dollars over 2024-34 and employment down 13.1%.
